literal: that which holds, supports, sustains; from √dhṛ 'to hold, to bear'

cognates.note: Skt. √dhṛ < PIE *dʰer- ‘hold, support’ is secure. The Latin firmus/fortis link to the same root is the traditional (Pokorny) reconstruction and is plausible but not universally accepted; Eng. ‘firm’ is a Latin loan, not a native cognate.

invariant_definition: *Dharma* is a load-bearing term across ritual theory, jurisprudence, ontology, yoga, and Buddhist phenomenology. The root sense is 'that which upholds,' but philosophical use immediately splits into several linked registers. In Mīmāṃsā, *dharma* is what is known through Vedic injunction and enacted through prescribed performance; the debate then turns on whether an act is *kratvartha* or *puruṣārtha*, and on how unseen efficacy connects action and result. In epic and social discourse, *dharma* becomes the right ordering of role, relation, and circumstance. In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, a *dharma* of a *dharmin* is a property borne by a substance. In Buddhism, *dharma* or *dhamma* can name both the teaching and the elementary factors analyzed as the constituents of experience. Vedānta inherits all these uses but reorders them around liberation: which *dharma* binds, which purifies, which continues as devotion, and whether the highest state lies beyond enjoinment or fulfills it.

school_framing.shared_core: Across schools that share the *śabda* *dharma*, the term names **that which upholds an order—ritual, ethical, ontological, or soteriological—and orients its bearer toward a highest end**. Mīmāṃsā fixes the *lakṣaṇa* as *codanā-lakṣaṇo 'rtho dharmaḥ* (Jaimini 1.1.2); Vaiśeṣika fixes it as *yataḥ abhyudaya-niḥśreyasa-siddhiḥ sa dharmaḥ* (Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra 1.1.1–2). Both keep the same structural role; the schools then differ on *which* order is load-bearing and on the *register* under which *dharma* speaks (ritual-normative, social, ontological-property, motion-medium, soteriological).
school_framing.register_axes_note: Schools differ along the register axis (RN / Eth / M / S) and the addressee axis (*adhikārin* of ritual vs. *mumukṣu* vs. *bhakta*), not on the underlying word's structural role. Genuine disagreements survive: Mādhyamaka denies the realist *dharma*-ontology that Vaiśeṣika asserts (AF1: same register LD/M, same scope-inside *dharma*-realism, real disagreement); Jainism's *dharmāstikāya* gives *dharma* a *different concept* — motion-medium *dravya*, not normative principle.

per_school.definition: Within Advaita, *svadharma* belongs to the preparatory economy of *adhikāra* and purification rather than to the immediate production of *mokṣa* [[BhR 1.1 ṭīkā 12](<cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.12>)]. Madhusūdana accordingly cites the Bhāgavata to show that exclusive Hari-bhajana outranks mere perseverance in one’s own duty, since dharma pursued as karma does not by itself culminate in the highest end [[BhR 1.1 ṭīkā 12](<cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.12>)].

*Dharma* retains binding reality because śāstra assigns distinct yet non-conflicting domains to Vedic and Pāñcarātra injunction, and Vedānta Deśika speaks of a normatively settled *dharma* governing their proper employment [[PR 1, Avirodhopayogābhyām](cite://vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.vaikhānasa-pāñcarātra-avirodha)]. At the existential level, Yāmuna treats mere *dharma-niṣṭhā* as insufficient unless it matures into surrender at the Lord’s feet, so dharma is preserved but gathered into *prapatti* and *bhagavad-śeṣatva* [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

Madhva uses *dharma* with full realist force: the *pakṣa*, *sapakṣa*, and *vipakṣa* are each specified through a borne property, so inferential practice tracks real *dharma*-relations rather than merely conceptual imputations [[MUK 239.15](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 15>)] [[MUK 239.16](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 16>)] [[MUK 239.17](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 17>)]. The same realism governs his anti-Buddhist polemic: the fault lies in treating *dharmas* as self-standing items, whereas their intelligibility depends upon the higher order of Viṣṇu’s governance and not upon isolated factor-ontology [[VTV 3.21](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.21)].

Within Acintya-Bhedābheda, *dharma* in the highest register is *parama-dharma*; Jīva glosses the term not as merely social or ritual observance but as *bhagavad-dhyānādi-lakṣaṇa*, the complex of practices centered on Bhagavān [[TS §19](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§19)]. Baladeva makes the school’s construal still more explicit by stating that the dharma taught there is characterized by *kṛṣṇa-saṅkīrtana*, so dharma reaches its proper form as name-centered bhakti rather than as an autonomous puruṣārtha-domain [[TS Ṭip. §23](cite://baladeva/tattva-sandarbha-tippani/§23)].

In Vallabha’s ontology, *dharma* is retained in the strong sense of a real property: *nāma-rūpa* are *kārya-dharmas*, predicates of the manifested effect and not illusory overlays on Brahman [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)] [[AB 1.1.4d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d)]. Because the effect is Brahman’s own manifestation under *avikṛta-pariṇāma*, dharma-language marks real determinations within manifestation without compromising pure non-duality [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)] [[AB 1.1.4d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d)].

In Sāṅkhya, *dharma* is one of the *bhāvas* that inhabit the *liṅga* and condition transmigration, together with jñāna, vairāgya, and aiśvarya [[SK 40](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/40)] [[SK 43](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/43)]. Its causal valence is explicit: dharma carries the stream upward, adharma downward, and only jñāna terminates the process in *apavarga* [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/44)].

In Yoga, *dharma* names the determinable trait-structure through which a *dharmī* is followed across change; the transformations of property, temporal mark, and state are generalized from citta to the elements and senses [[YS 3.13](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.13)] [[YS 3.14](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14)]. At the soteriological limit, however, the culminating concentration is *dharma-megha-samādhi*, where even attachment to discriminative knowledge is relinquished and the path stands on the threshold of final release [[YS 4.29](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.29)].

Kaṇāda opens Vaiśeṣika by defining *dharma* as that from which *abhyudaya* and *niḥśreyasa* are accomplished, so the term first names a real causal-normative principle and not merely social duty [[VS 1.1.1](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.1)] [[VS 1.1.2](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.2)]. Within the padārtha-analysis that follows, explanatory order is carried by *dravya*, *guṇa*, and *karma*; a guṇa is an *aguṇavat* attribute resting in a substance, which is why Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika can speak naturally of a *dharma* of a *dharmin* without leaving its ontological register [[VS 1.1.15](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.15)] [[VS 1.1.16](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16)].

Jaimini fixes *dharma* as the very object of inquiry: one turns to Mīmāṃsā not toward a highest substance but toward the ascertainment of what is to be done [[MS 1.1.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.1)]. Its lakṣaṇa is *codanā-lakṣaṇo 'rtho dharmaḥ*, and because dharma is rooted in *śabda* the non-verbal is not to be expected as an independent source for it [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)].

Nāgārjuna presses the term *dharma* to the point of de-reification: no dharma can be shown to arise as existent, non-existent, or both, and therefore no substantialist model of causal support survives analysis [[MMK 1.7](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.7)] [[MMK 1.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.8)] [[MMK 1.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.9)]. Buddha-dharma itself is then articulated through the two truths: conventionally there is *dharmadeśanā*, but ultimately no dharma arises independently and therefore none is non-empty [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)] [[MMK 24.12](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.12)] [[MMK 24.19](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19)]. When all dharmas are empty, nirvāṇa too is the pacification of all proliferations rather than the disclosure of a final dharma-substrate [[MMK 25.22](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.22)] [[MMK 25.24](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.24)] [[MMK 18.7](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7)].

Vasubandhu treats ordinary imputations of self and dharma as moments internal to *vijñāna-pariṇāma*; what appears as a field of discrete dharmas is already representation-only [[Triṃś 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)]. The school’s decisive move is *dharma-nairātmya*: the grāhya-grāhaka structure imagined in dharmas is denied as *kalpitātman*, not by abolishing all appearance but by redescribing it through the three natures and the threefold *niḥsvabhāvatā* [[Viṃś 10](<cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *Dharma-Nairātmya*>)] [[Triṃś 23](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/23)]. On that basis the ultimate among dharmas is *tathatā*, while the consummation of the path is spoken of as *dharmakāya* [[Triṃś 25](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/25)] [[Triṃś 30](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30)].

In Trika, dharma is first internalized: Abhinavagupta states that the abiding dharma of the entire tattva-cluster is ātman itself, immersed in Śiva’s nectar, so the term names the self-manifest order of consciousness rather than an external norm over against it [[TA 1.82-84](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.82-84)]. The same analysis refuses a property-bearer standing apart from its properties, for the earth-element appears through the totality of its dharmas and therefore no dharmin distinct from dharma remains to be posited; dharma-language here tracks manifest powers and aspects within non-dual appearing [[TA 9.291-294](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/9.291-294)]. At the practical level, Abhinavagupta distinguishes the *lokadharmī* from the *śivadharmī* adept, and defines the latter’s initiation precisely as that which removes *loka-dharma*; dharma is thus reconfigured from conventional norm-governance into the liberative discipline proper to Śaiva dīkṣā [[TA 15.23-29](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.23-29)].

Jainism gives *dharma* a sharply technical sense: alongside *adharma*, *ākāśa*, and *pudgala*, it is one of the *ajīva-kāyas* and therefore one of the real dravyas of the cosmos [[TS 5.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.1)] [[TS 5.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.2)]. Its distinctive function is not merit but motion-medium: *dharma* aids movement, *adharma* aids rest, and the liberated soul’s upward movement is possible because the *dharmāstikāya* exists [[TS 5.17](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.17)] [[TS 10.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.8)].

textual_history.summary: *Dharma* is the abstract noun on √dhṛ, 'to hold, uphold, sustain' — 'that which upholds'. Its Vedic form is the neuter *dhárman*, and there it names the fixed ordinances by which the cosmos and the rite are held in place, closely allied to *ṛta*, the cosmic-and-ritual Order. In the Ṛgveda the gods 'uphold the dharmas' as Viṣṇu strides out the worlds (*ato dharmāṇi dhārayan*, RV 1.22.18), and the primal ordinances are explicitly the first acts of sacrifice — 'the gods sacrificed the sacrifice with the sacrifice; those were the first dharmas' (RV 10.90.16 = 1.164.50). This ritual-ordinance sense hardens, in the Brāhmaṇa-and-Mīmāṃsā line, into *dharma* as Vedically-enjoined duty: Jaimini's foundational definition makes it precisely *codanā-lakṣaṇo 'rtho dharmaḥ*, 'dharma is the good [/object] characterised by [Vedic] injunction' (Mīmāṃsā-sūtra 1.1.2), and the Chāndogya already counts the 'three branches of dharma' (sacrifice, study, charity, and the rest, ChU 2.23.1). Alongside this an *ethical* sense rises in the Upaniṣads: the Taittirīya enjoins 'speak the truth, practise dharma' (TU 1.11.1), and the Bṛhadāraṇyaka makes dharma the supreme ruling principle above even the warrior-power and identifies it with truth — 'that dharma is truth itself' (BĀU 1.4.14). The *Gītā* then inflects dharma toward the social-and-existential: *sva-dharma*, one's own duty of station, whose abandonment is sin (2.31–33), and the cosmic *dharma-saṃsthāpana*, the Lord's periodic re-establishment of dharma when it decays (4.7–8). The arc *ṛta*→*dharman*→*dharma* is partly genealogical (the Vedic *dhárman* is the literal etymological and conceptual ancestor of the later noun) and partly a real semantic broadening, as the word migrates from cosmic-ritual ordinance to enjoined duty to ethical order to station-bound obligation.

stages.sanskrit: trīṇi padā vi cakrame viṣṇur gopā adābhyaḥ | ato dharmāṇi dhārayan
stages.rendering: Three steps Viṣṇu strode out, the unassailable guardian — thereby upholding the dharmas (dharmāṇi dhārayan).
stages.note: The earliest pole, with the etymology visible on the surface: *dharmāṇi dhārayan*, 'upholding the dharmas', from √dhṛ. *Dharman* (neuter) here are the fixed ordinances or foundations that Viṣṇu's world-measuring strides hold in place — cosmic order rather than human duty. The word is the act-noun of holding, and its first objects are the supports of the world itself.

stages.sanskrit: yajñena yajñam ayajanta devās tāni dharmāṇi prathamāny āsan | te ha nākam mahimānaḥ sacanta yatra pūrve sādhyāḥ santi devāḥ
stages.rendering: With the sacrifice the gods sacrificed the sacrifice; those were the first dharmas (dharmāṇi prathamāni). The mighty ones reached the firmament where the ancient gods, the Sādhyas, are.
stages.note: *Dharman* as primal ordinance is here explicitly *ritual*: the first dharmas are the gods' own acts of sacrifice, the archetypal rites by which order was first established. This is the seam between the cosmic *ṛta* and the later Mīmāṃsaka *dharma* of enjoined sacrificial duty: the rite is, from the start, the load-bearing ordinance. (The same verse recurs at RV 1.164.50; we cite the Puruṣa-sūkta locus as the more familiar.)

stages.sanskrit: trayo dharmaskandhāḥ | yajño 'dhyayanaṃ dānam iti prathamaḥ | tapa eva dvitīyaḥ | brahmacāryācāryakulavāsī tṛtīyo 'tyantam ātmānam ācāryakule 'vasādayan | sarva ete puṇyalokā bhavanti | brahmasaṃstho 'mṛtatvam eti
stages.rendering: There are three branches of dharma. Sacrifice, study, and almsgiving — that is the first. Austerity alone is the second. The student of sacred knowledge dwelling in the teacher's house, mortifying himself utterly in the teacher's house — [that is] the third. All these attain the worlds of merit; [but] the one grounded in Brahman attains immortality.
stages.note: *Dharma* as a classifiable scheme of prescribed observances — the ritual-and-duty sense, here organised into 'branches' (*skandha*). Note the Upaniṣadic reservation already at work: these dharmas win the 'worlds of merit' (*puṇya-loka*), but it is the one 'grounded in Brahman' who reaches immortality — duty-dharma is subordinated to the knowledge of Brahman, anticipating the Vedāntic reordering.

stages.sanskrit: satyaṃ vada | dharmaṃ cara | svādhyāyān mā pramadaḥ | … | satyān na pramaditavyam | dharmān na pramaditavyam
stages.rendering: Speak the truth. Practise dharma (dharmaṃ cara). Do not neglect your study. … From truth do not swerve. From dharma do not swerve.
stages.note: The graduating teacher's exhortation, and the clearest early *ethical* use: *dharmaṃ cara*, 'practise dharma', set beside truth-telling and study as a way of life to be 'not swerved from'. Śaṅkara, commenting, glosses *dharma* here as 'the general term for what is to be done' (*anuṣṭheyānāṃ sāmānya-vacanam*) — dharma as right conduct in the broad sense, no longer only the rite.

stages.sanskrit: tac chreyo rūpam atyasṛjata dharmam | tad etat kṣatrasya kṣatraṃ yad dharmaḥ | tasmād dharmāt paraṃ nāsti | … | yo vai sa dharmaḥ satyaṃ vai tat | tasmāt satyaṃ vadantam āhur dharmaṃ vadatīti
stages.rendering: [Brahman] emitted a still better form, dharma. This dharma is the ruling-power of the ruling-power (kṣatrasya kṣatram); therefore there is nothing higher than dharma. … That which is dharma is truth itself (satyam); therefore of one who speaks the truth they say, 'he speaks dharma'.
stages.note: Dharma raised to the supreme normative principle — 'the kṣatra of the kṣatra', that which governs even the warrior-power, 'than which there is nothing higher' — and explicitly identified with *satya*, truth. This is the high-water mark of the *ṛta*→*dharma*→*satya* nexus: the Vedic cosmic Order, now internalised as the truth-grounded moral law that rules even kings. The genealogy from *ṛta* is conceptual rather than verbal (the word *ṛta* is not used here), but the role — the order above all powers — is the same.

stages.rendering: Dharma is the good (artha) characterised by [Vedic] injunction (codanā).
stages.note: The ritual-ordinance sense made into a formal definition: dharma is precisely whatever the Vedic injunctive utterance (*codanā*) enjoins, knowable only through *śabda* and not through perception (cf. MS 1.1.4–5). This is the Mīmāṃsaka crystallisation of the Brāhmaṇa duty-sense — dharma as enjoined performance — and the foil against which the Gītā's *sva-dharma* and Vedānta's subordination of duty to knowledge are both defined.

stages.sanskrit: sva-dharmam api cāvekṣya na vikampitum arhasi | dharmyād dhi yuddhāc chreyo 'nyat kṣatriyasya na vidyate || … || atha cet tvam imaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃgrāmaṃ na kariṣyasi | tataḥ sva-dharmaṃ kīrtiṃ ca hitvā pāpam avāpsyasi
stages.rendering: Looking too to your own dharma (sva-dharma), you ought not to waver; for there is no greater good for a warrior than a war consonant with dharma. … || But if you will not wage this dharma-consonant war, then, forsaking your own dharma and your honour, you will incur sin.
stages.note: The *Gītā*'s distinctive inflection: *sva-dharma*, the duty proper to one's own station (here the warrior's), whose performance is obligatory and whose abandonment is *pāpa*, sin. Dharma is now station-bound social-and-moral obligation — neither the Vedic cosmic ordinance nor the bare Mīmāṃsaka rite, though Śaṅkara is at pains (on 2.33) to read the warrior's *sva-dharma* as not-adharma even when it involves killing.

stages.sanskrit: yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata | abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmy aham || paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām | dharma-saṃsthāpanārthāya saṃbhavāmi yuge yuge
stages.rendering: Whenever there is a decline of dharma, Bhārata, and an uprising of adharma, then I send forth myself. || For the protection of the good and the destruction of the evil-doers, for the firm re-establishment of dharma (dharma-saṃsthāpana), I come into being age after age.
stages.note: Dharma at its widest in the *Gītā*: the cosmic-moral order whose periodic decline the Lord descends to restore (*dharma-saṃsthāpana*). Here the term has travelled the full distance from the Vedic *dhárman* — the ordinance that upholds the world — to a moral-and-cosmic order that the divine itself underwrites and repairs, the load-bearing sense of √dhṛ now carried by the personal Lord. Śaṅkara glosses *dharma* here as 'characterised by the [system of] varṇa and āśrama' and as 'the means of beings' welfare and highest good'.

translator_note: Avoid the lazy glosses 'religion,' 'duty,' and 'law' when used without qualification. 'Religion' imports a modern confessional category; 'duty' narrows the term to ethics; 'law' can make it sound merely juridical. *Dharma* names whatever upholds a normative, ontological, or soteriological order, and context decides which register is active.
